entities.name: Munich Handbook (CLM 849)

attributes.description: Medieval necromantic manual containing spirit lists and conjurations

entities.raw_quote: The most sustained discussion of demons in the Munich manual is an extended list which names and describes eleven spirits (no. 34).

entities.name: Oriens

attributes.title: rex Orientis / altissimus et benignissime rex

entities.raw_quote: Oriens (or rex Orientis) (altissimus et benignissimus rex, no. 4; benign[issim]e, no. 41)

entities.name: Astaroth
entities.raw_quote: Astaroth (or Astoroth or Astarotht) (demon malignus, no. 2; no. 12; no. 33; princeps demoniorum, no. 38)

entities.name: Belial
entities.raw_quote: Belial (inicialis princeps, no. 12)

entities.name: Berith

titles: fornicator, temptator, et seductor, possessor hominum

entities.raw_quote: Berith (or Berit) (no. 1; spiritus potens, magnificus, et illustris, no. 11; princeps demoniorum, fornicator, temptator, et seductor, possessor hominum, no. 38)

entities.name: Belzebub
entities.raw_quote: Belzebub (or Belzebuc?) (no. 1; princeps demoniorum, no. 38)

entities.name: Lucifer
entities.raw_quote: Lucifer (no. 1)

entities.name: Satan / Sathan
entities.raw_quote: Satan (no. 1; demon [from cardinal direction], no. 27)

entities.name: Hanni
entities.raw_quote: Hanni comes in a fiery flame, but also takes human form.

entities.name: Curson

attributes.function: reveals divine and hidden matters, even regarding divinity and creation

entities.raw_quote: Curson is said readily to assume a human and aerial body; presumably the others as well take on aerial bodies when they come visibly.

entities.name: Bartha
entities.raw_quote: Bartha (or Bartham?) (rex, no. 15)

entities.name: Baltim
entities.raw_quote: Baltim (or Balthym? or Balthim or Baltym) (dux, no. 15)

entities.name: Saltim
entities.raw_quote: Saltim (dux, no. 15)

entities.name: Galtim
entities.raw_quote: Galtim (or Galtym? or Galtyra?) (dux, no. 15)

entities.name: Lytim
entities.raw_quote: Lytim (demon, no. 39)

entities.name: Le livre des esperitz
entities.raw_quote: Identical in form but independent in content is the list contained in Le livre des esperitz found in a fifteenth-century French manuscript (Trinity College, Cambridge, MS 0.8.29).

entities.name: Bezlebuth
entities.raw_quote: The service Bezlebuth performs, which would have endeared him to Faustus, is to reveal all the secrets of hell.

entities.name: Asmoday
entities.raw_quote: ...including Asmoday (a great king with three heads, who furnishes a ring of virtues, teaches mathematics, answers questions truly, makes a person invisible, reveals hidden treasure...

entities.name: Testament of Solomon
entities.raw_quote: These catalogues were especially common in the treatises and grimoires of the early modern era... but the basic idea can be traced to medieval sources.

entities.name: Pseudomonarchia daemonum

attributes.author: Johannes Weyer
attributes.spirits_count: sixty-nine demons with offices and functions

entities.raw_quote: ...(Johannes Weyer’s Pseudomonarchia daemonum lists sixty-nine demons with their offices and functions)

entities.name: Lemegeton
entities.raw_quote: The Lemegeton lists seventy-two demons with their functions, including Asmoday...

entities.name: Liber officiorum

attributes.content: catalogues four emperors and various kings, dukes, marquises, and counts

entities.raw_quote: Trithemius speaks of a Liber officiorum that catalogues four emperors and various kings, duces, marquises, and counts.

entities.name: Rawlinson MS

attributes.content: contains instructions for striking spirits outside circle

entities.raw_quote: The Rawlinson manuscript says at one juncture that if the spirit refuses to withdraw, the master should trace a circle with the point of his sword and then ‘strike’ the spirit who is left standing outside the circle.

entities.name: Tobit (Book of Tobias)

attributes.function: demon expelling via fish liver fumigation

entities.raw_quote: When Tobias placed a fish’s liver on coals, demons were expelled (Tob 6) – but the book of Tobias is not canonical...

entities.name: Roger Bacon (necromantic treatise ascribed to)

attributes.warning: demons try to frighten magicians with illusions, hissing, and terrifying shapes

entities.raw_quote: ...the treatise on necromancy ascribed to Roger Bacon warns that demons will try to frighten the magician with illusions...

entities.name: Paris theologians (1398)

attributes.condemned: belief that demons can be coerced by magical arts

entities.raw_quote: ...the theologians at the University of Paris had in 1398 condemned the belief that... demons are compelled to speak truthfully.

entities.name: Charlemagne’s Admonitio generalis (789)
entities.raw_quote: In 789, Charlemagne’s Admonitio generalis condemned the invocation of ‘unknown angels’, presumably for the same reason.

entities.name: Johann von Frankfurt

attributes.view: demons cannot be coerced; they come fraudulently, not by compulsion

entities.raw_quote: Johann von Frankfurt noted in the early fifteenth century that Plato distinguished between good daimons (calodemones) and evil (cacodemones), but for scriptural and dogmatic reasons he rejects this distinction...

summary: This chunk details the demonology of the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), especially its list of eleven named spirits with ranks, appearances, and functions. It compares these to similar lists in Le livre des esperitz and later grimoires like Pseudomonarchia daemonum and Lemegeton, tracing roots to Testament of Solomon. The text also discusses medieval theological debates over demonic coercion, corporeality, and appearance—especially the insistence that demons cannot be compelled but come fraudulently—and references key figures like Johann von Frankfurt and institutions like the Paris theologians (1398).
key_passages: The most sustained discussion of demons in the Munich manual is an extended list which names and describes eleven spirits (no. 34). Each has a rank: marquis, ‘president’ (preses), count, duke, prince or king.
They may be able to take on any appearance they wish, conceivably even that of a famed beauty such as Helen of Troy... but it seems to have been agreed that their most natural shape is terrifying.
Johann von Frankfurt noted in the early fifteenth century... He insists that demons cannot be coerced: they are free agents, with intellect and will, and cannot be bound by human actions...
The service Bezlebuth performs, which would have endeared him to Faustus, is to reveal all the secrets of hell.
Identical in form but independent in content is the list contained in Le livre des esperitz found in a fifteenth-century French manuscript (Trinity College, Cambridge, MS 0.8.29).
